title: Shearing a sheep with hand shears
subtitle: The blade method for small-scale DIY shearing.
excerpt: Hand shears are slower than electric clippers but quiet, portable, and the right tool for a keeper with two or three sheep who wants to shear their own animals without contracting a professional. The sequence is the same as electric blade shearing: sit the sheep on its rump, work from the brisket rearward, and keep the skin taut.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb shearing guidance, ahdb.org.uk. British Wool Board, britishwool.org.uk, shearing training resources. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep (2023).

glossaryTerms.definition: A fine break in the wool staple that forms when new growth pushes last year's fleece upward from the skin surface. Shearing into the rise (when the gap is visible) produces a cleaner cut and a more complete fleece removal.

Removing heavily soiled or matted wool around the breech, tail, and hind legs before shearing. Done with dagging shears or hand shears 4 to 6 weeks before main shearing to prevent flystrike during the transition.

content.text: Shearing by hand takes 15 to 30 minutes per sheep for a competent beginner working alone, compared with 3 to 5 minutes for an experienced electric-shearer. For a flock of two or three, the pace is acceptable, the equipment cost is minimal, and the skill builds with each season. Shear when the
is visible and the weather is settled warm, typically late May through June for UK lowland flocks.
Hold the sheep off feed for 12 hours before shearing, a full rumen makes the sheep heavier and more prone to bloat when sat on its rump. Do the
on a separate day at least a week before if the breech area is heavily soiled. Sharpen the shears before you begin; blunt shears pull rather than cut and fatigue the shearing hand quickly.

technique. Tilt it onto its rump by placing one hand under the chin and the other on the flank, rotating it backward into a sitting position. A sheep sitting on its rump is passive, the rumen compresses slightly and the animal stops struggling. Work on a clean surface: a rubber mat or a wooden board keeps the fleece clean.

Start at the brisket (chest) and work in long blows toward the belly. Keep the skin taut with the free hand, pulling the skin tight in the direction opposite the shear stroke. Work down the left belly, around the crotch, up the left flank, and over the back. Roll the sheep onto its other side (keep it sitting by moving behind it) and repeat on the right. The fleece should come away as a single piece.

Skin cuts happen on loose or folded skin at the elbows, udder, and belly. Apply a light spray of iodine or wound spray immediately and check the cut is shallow. Deep cuts need veterinary attention. Most beginner cuts are superficial, the skin is tougher than it looks. Blunt shears increase cut risk by requiring more pressure.

A freshly shorn sheep is vulnerable to cold and sunburn. Do not shear sheep less than three weeks before a predicted cold snap. Apply a purple wound spray or iodine to any cuts immediately after releasing the animal. Return the sheep to the flock straight away; an isolated shorn sheep calls for the others and stresses quickly.
